entry.title: Re: Can you create subcontracting Purchase Orders with Se...
entry.content: I have not seen this scenario specifically but I believe it's possible.  Why I believe it's possible is that: you can start a subcontracting PO with a purchase requisition and you can initiate a purchase requisition from within a Sales Order  - the "drop ship/sell through" sales process initiates a Purchase Requisition via the sales order line item control key.  The new service management process starts with a "service order" which is a Sales Order.

entry.title: Cross-Plant Actual Cost Roll-up
entry.content:
We are facing a question regarding GROW standard capabilities and will appreciate your insights.

Business Scenario

  • We have Plant A and Plant B.
  • Plant A manufactures a semifinished product.
  • Plant B procures this semifinished product from Plant A to assemble the Finished Product.

Question

After running CKMLCP, can we achieve a Cross-Plant Actual Cost Roll-up in system?  Can we see cost break down from Plant B?

entry.title: How SAP Situation Handling Automates Issue Detection & Empowers Your Team?
entry.content:
In today's fast-paced business environment, critical issues—like an expiring contract or an un-billed sales order—can slip through the cracks, leading to revenue loss, added costs, and operational delays. What if your Cloud ERP system could automatically find these problems and notify the right person at the right time?

In this session replay from our AI Readiness Service, Knut Manske , Engineering Lead for SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP Foundation, demystifies SAP Situation Handling. He moves beyond theory to show you exactly how this powerful, license-included framework works to transform reactive firefighting into proactive business management. 

Core Value Proposition: The "Why" of Situation Handling

Situation Handling is designed to recognize issues requiring attention at the right moment and provide the right information to the right person.​ It acts as an automated watchdog for your business processes, eliminating the need for users to manually sift through reports and lists to find problems.

Key Benefits Highlighted in the Session:
  • Proactive, Not Reactive:  The system automatically detects issues in the background (e.g., budgets exceeded, contracts expiring, confirmations pending) before they cause damage.
  • Context-Aware Notifications: Responsible users are informed via SAP Fiori notifications, email, SAP Mobile Start, and even within Microsoft Teams, with direct links to resolve the issue.
  • Unified View for Faster Resolution: A dedicated "Situation Page" consolidates all relevant data from different sources, giving users the full context they need to act immediately.
  • Low-Code Flexibility: Start fast with 140+ pre-delivered use cases, or use the low-code, extended framework to build and customize your own scenarios without extensive development.
  • Foundation for AI: This is the core infrastructure that enables upcoming AI-driven recommendations, where the system can suggest actions based on company guidelines (planned for 2026).
 
See it in Action: Main Use Cases Demonstrated
  • The Everyday Overview: See All Your Issues in One Place (Video Timestamp: 18:54 - 20:58)
  • From Alert to Action in One Click (Video Timestamp: 22:02 - 22:55)
  • Activate a Pre-Built Use Case in Minutes (Video Timestamp: 30:31 - 38:47)
  • Deep Dive: Finance & GR/IR Reconciliation (Video Timestamp: 39:40 - 44:15)

Watch the full session replay​ to get the complete picture, including the live Q&A where Knut and product manager Ankitkumar Kaneri  answer specific questions. SAP Partners and Employee could access SAP Partner Portal for session replay and slides access.

entry.title: Re: How to Change Super Administrators in SAP for Me

Typically, your company’s current Super Administrator can assign Super User rights to another user via the Copy User's Authorization function within Users & Contacts - SAP for Me.
Therefore, could you kindly reach out to your existing Super Administrator to complete this action for you.

Add Super Administrator - An existing Super Administrator must provide all authorizations to another S-user ID.
Tip: Follow KBA 1614304 to copy or clone their own authorizations.

For more detailed information, please refer to the following KBAs:

2596214 - How to maintain Super, Cloud, and User Administrator S-user IDs listed in My Important Con... 

1282808 - What tasks is a Super, Cloud, or User Administrator S-user ID responsible for? - SAP for M...

If you have any questions or need further assistance, please feel free to contact us.

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: changes to company org chart and sync with jobinfo
entry.content:
Hi @user116 

The association with Department and Division is at master data level and which wont auto update in the employee data level. 

If the existing association is removed between Department and Division and if you view the information at Job information or position still you shall able to view the Department and Division mapped even though the association between  Department and Division is removed at master data level. When you try to edit the position or job information then you shall able to view Division and not the department. During edit action the associations are validated. 

New association will work at "on-change" action. So you have to either re-import or manually update the department information in position or job information.

 

Thanks & Regards

Arun

entry.title: Grounding AI in Reality: How We Made Joule Answer From Our Own Documents
entry.content:
There’s a moment in most AI projects where you realize the model confidently gave you a completely wrong answer — and you have no idea how to fix it. You can’t retrain it, you can’t shout at it, and you definitely can’t ship it to users like that.

That was roughly where we were when we started experimenting with Document Grounding on SAP BTP. We wanted Joule (SAP’s AI assistant) to answer questions from a specific equipment manual — not from its general training data, but from our actual PDF. This post is about how we got there.

What We Were Trying to Do

The goal was simple on paper: take a technical PDF (an operator manual for a loader), upload it to SAP’s Document Grounding service, and let Joule search through it when users ask questions. Instead of guessing or hallucinating, Joule would pull actual passages from the document and answer based on those.

The underlying pattern is called RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation. You store your documents as vectors (mathematical representations of meaning), and when a question comes in, you find the most semantically relevant chunks and feed them to the LLM as context. It’s become the standard way to make AI useful on private knowledge bases.

SAP BTP has its own managed infrastructure for this through the Document Grounding service, and there’s a Python library called langchain-rage that wraps its API in a LangChain-compatible interface.

Prerequisites

Before you try to replicate this, you’ll need:

  • An SAP BTP account with the Document Grounding service instance provisioned
  • Identity service set up with X.509 certificate support (this is what handles authentication)
  • Service keys generated from both the Document Grounding service and the Identity service — you’ll need these as JSON files locally
  • Python 3.11+ — we used 3.11.14
  • A PDF you want to search — for our test we used a 114-page equipment manual
  • cf CLI access to pull fresh service keys if needed
  • Joule Studio access if you want to test the end-to-end conversational integration

How the Pieces Fit Together

Before diving into setup, here’s the high-level picture:

Your PDF
   ↓
Load pages with PyMuPDF
   ↓
Split into ~1000-character chunks (LangChain splitter)
   ↓
Upload to BTP Document Grounding (vector store)
   ↓
BTP auto-generates embeddings for each chunk
   ↓
User asks a question in Joule or your app
   ↓
Semantic search finds the most relevant chunks
   ↓
LLM generates a grounded, cited answer

The service handles the embedding generation — you don’t need to bring your own embedding model or manage any of that infrastructure. That’s honestly one of the nicer aspects of this approach.

Setting Up the Environment

# Create and activate a virtual environment
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

# Install dependencies
pip install langchain-community langchain-openai pymupdf requests-toolbelt

# Install the SAP RAGe LangChain integration (from internal git)
pip install git+https://<your-internal-repo>/langchain-rage.git

Place your service key files in the project root: - doc-grounding-service-key-fresh.json — from your Document Grounding service instance - identity-key-x509-fresh.json — from your Identity service instance (must include X.509 certs)

If your service keys are more than a day or two old, regenerate them via CF CLI. Stale keys were a source of several confusing authentication failures for us.

Step 1: Create the Collection

A “collection” is the vector store bucket that holds your document chunks. The key thing to get right is the metadata — specifically setting type=custom. Without this, Joule won’t discover the collection when grounding responses.

from langchain_rage.rage_clients.vector.client import VectorClient

vector_client = VectorClient.create_from_service_keys(
    path_document_grounding_key="doc-grounding-service-key-fresh.json",
    path_identity_key="identity-key-x509-fresh.json"
)

# Create the collection
collection = vector_client.create_collection(
    title="my-document-collection",
    metadata={"type": "custom"}  # Required for Joule integration
)

collection_id = collection.id
print(f"Collection created: {collection_id}")

Save that collection ID somewhere — you’ll reference it in every subsequent operation.

 

Step 2: Ingest Your PDF

This is where most of the work happens. We load the PDF with PyMuPDF (which preserves page numbers in metadata — important for citations), split into chunks, convert to the RAGe format, and upload.

from langchain_community.document_loaders import DirectoryLoader, PyMuPDFLoader
from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_rage.rage_clients.vector.model import (
    Chunk as RAGeChunk,
    Document as RAGeDocument,
    MetaData as RAGeMetaData
)
import time

# Load
loader = DirectoryLoader(path=".", glob="*.pdf", loader_cls=PyMuPDFLoader)
raw_documents = loader.load()

# Split
chunker = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=1000,
    chunk_overlap=100
)

# Convert and upload in batches
batch_size = 50
all_ids = []

for raw_doc in raw_documents:
    chunks = chunker.split_documents([raw_doc])
    rage_docs = []

    for chunk in chunks:
        rage_chunk = RAGeChunk(
            content=chunk.page_content,
            metadata=[
                RAGeMetaData(key=k, value=[str(v)])
                for k, v in chunk.metadata.items()
            ]
        )
        rage_doc = RAGeDocument(
            chunks=[rage_chunk],
            metadata=[RAGeMetaData(key="title", value=[raw_doc.metadata["source"]])]
        )
        rage_docs.append(rage_doc)

    # Upload in batches
    for i in range(0, len(rage_docs), batch_size):
        batch = rage_docs[i:i + batch_size]
        try:
            ids = vector_client.create_documents(
                collection_id=collection_id,
                documents=batch
            )
            all_ids.extend(ids)
            time.sleep(1)  # Be nice to the stage environment
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Batch failed: {e}")
            continue

print(f"Uploaded {len(all_ids)} chunks")

About chunk sizing: We settled on 1000 characters with 100 character overlap. Smaller chunks give more precise retrieval but miss broader context; larger chunks are the opposite. 1000 works well for technical documentation with a mix of lists, tables, and prose. You’ll likely need to tune this for your content type.

About batching: If you’re on stage/canary, do not try to upload everything in one shot. We lost entire uploads that way. Batches of 50 with a 1-second delay between them worked reliably. If you’re resuming a partial upload, drop to batches of 10 and add a 3-second delay.

For a 114-page PDF, we ended up with 233 chunks total. The whole upload took a few minutes with batching.

Step 3: Verify the Collection

Before moving on, it’s worth checking that the upload actually worked:

status = vector_client.get_collection(collection_id=collection_id)
print(f"Collection status: {status}")
You can also run a quick semantic query to verify the chunks are searchable:
from langchain_rage.rage_clients.retrieval.client import RetrievalClient
from langchain_rage.rage_clients.retrieval.model import Search, SearchFilter, SearchConfiguration

retrieval_client = RetrievalClient.create_from_service_keys(
    path_document_grounding_key="doc-grounding-service-key-fresh.json",
    path_identity_key="identity-key-x509-fresh.json"
)

search = Search(
    query="maintenance intervals",
    filters=[SearchFilter(
        dataRepositories=[collection_id],
        dataRepositoryType="vector",
        searchConfiguration=SearchConfiguration(maxChunkCount=3)
    )]
)

results = retrieval_client.query(search).json()

If you get chunks back with relevance scores in the 0.4–0.6 range, you’re in good shape. Scores above 0.5 on a meaningful query indicate solid embedding quality.

Step 4: Joule Integration

Once your collection is live, connecting it to Joule requires a deployment through Joule Studio. The key configuration is the capability definition — specifically making sure the capability points to your collection and is marked with the right metadata so Joule’s grounding layer can discover it.

The deployment manifest structure looks roughly like:

# da.sapdas.yaml (simplified)
apiVersion: sapdas/v1
kind: DeploymentAssistant
metadata:
  name: my-document-grounding-capability
spec:
  capabilities:
    - path: a2a/
# a2a/capability.sapdas.yaml (simplified)
apiVersion: sapdas/v3
kind: Capability
metadata:
  name: document-search
spec:
  grounding:
    collections:
      - id: "<your-collection-id>"
        type: custom

After deploying via Joule Studio’s CLI, Joule will automatically use your collection when users ask questions that match its scope. The grounding is transparent — users just see answers, and Joule cites the source pages.

What It Looks Like in Practice

Once everything is connected, the experience is straightforward. A user asks Joule something like “what’s the maintenance schedule for the first 100 operating hours?” — Joule runs a semantic search against the collection, finds the relevant passages (in our case from pages 62 and 76–77 of the manual), and generates a response grounded in those chunks.

The relevance scores we saw in testing:

Query

Score Range

Source Pages

“maintenance intervals”

0.50 – 0.56

62, 76–77

“safety requirements before operation”

0.45 – 0.50

18

“engine oil change procedure”

0.50 – 0.55

76–77

“technical specifications”

0.48 – 0.54

30

Not all queries will score high — if a topic isn’t covered in your document, scores drop noticeably (below 0.3) and Joule will typically indicate it couldn’t find relevant information. That failure mode is actually what you want: grounded AI that knows what it doesn’t know.

 

 

A Few Things We’d Do Differently

Start with a resume-capable ingestion script. We wrote ingest_pdf.py first (one big upload), then ingest_pdf_batched.py (batched), then ingest_pdf_resume.py (resume from checkpoint). We should have started with the third one. Save your uploaded document IDs to a file as you go, and on retry, skip chunks that already made it through.

Test semantic search before deploying to Joule. We spent time debugging the Joule integration when the actual problem was that some chunks didn’t upload. A simple retrieval test catches this early.

Keep your service keys fresh. X.509 certificates have expiry dates. If you hit sudden authentication failures days later, regenerating keys is the first thing to try.

Name your collections descriptively. We used joule-document-test which was fine for a PoC but would get confusing fast in a real environment with multiple document sets.

Wrapping Up

Document grounding is one of those features where the demo works immediately and then you discover all the small things that make it production-ready: batch handling, error recovery, chunk size tuning, collection discoverability. The SAP BTP Document Grounding service handles the hard parts (embedding generation, vector storage, similarity search) reasonably well — the langchain-rage integration makes it approachable from Python without dealing with raw API calls.

If you’re building anything where you need AI to answer reliably from a known corpus — internal documentation, product manuals, compliance documents — this pattern is worth understanding. It’s not magic, but it’s close enough.

Setup based on SAP BTP Document Grounding service . The langchain-rage library is an internal SAP integration library for LangChain. Joule Studio is required for the conversational integration step.

entry.title: Extending SAP Joule with Custom DAS Capabilities
entry.content:
SAP Joule's Digital Assistant Service (DAS) enables extending Joule with custom capabilities: custom scenarios, functions, and backend integrations that respond to natural language queries. On paper, it sounds straightforward: define the scenarios in YAML, compile, deploy, and Joule starts answering domain-specific questions.

In practice, getting a custom DAS capability to work end-to-end: from a user's question in Work Zone all the way to a backend API response: involves navigating a chain of components across multiple subaccounts, each with its own quirks and undocumented constraints.

This post captures the real issues we hit while building a custom Joule integration and the fixes that got us through.

The Architecture: What we are Building

Before diving into the issues, it helps to understand the full request flow. When a user asks Joule a question, the request traverses multiple layers:

User (Work Zone / Standalone Joule)
        │
        ▼
DAS Runtime (Joule subaccount)
  1. Preselection → matches the scenario via semantic similarity
  2. Executes the DAS function
  3. Resolves destination
  4. POSTs to the backend API
        │
        ▼  via destination (OAuth2ClientCredentials)
Backend Service (application subaccount)
  1. Parses request, resolves entity references
  2. Processes business logic
  3. Calls downstream services (AI, DB, etc.)
        │
        ▼
Downstream Service
  Processes and returns response

Each arrow in this diagram is a potential failure point. The DAS runtime, the backend, and any downstream services live in different subaccounts with different networking and authentication contexts. This is where things get interesting.

Issue 1: DAS Compilation: The Hidden Limits

joule compile has strict validation rules that aren't always obvious.

Timeout ceiling: The api-request action type has a maximum timeout of 45 seconds. Set it to 60 and compilation fails silently with a generic validation error. If the backend needs more time, the API needs optimization rather than a longer timeout.

Description length limit: The response_context description fields in scenario YAMLs have a 50 character limit. This is easy to exceed when trying to be descriptive:

# This will fail compilation: too long
- value: $target_result.currentContent
  description: The current content of the section before the proposed edit is applied

# This works: under 50 characters
- value: $target_result.currentContent
  description: Current content before the edit

Issue 2: "I Don't Understand": When Preselection Ignores the Scenario

This was the most ambiguous issue. Everything compiled and deployed successfully, but Joule kept responding with "I don't understand" or routing queries to SAP Help instead of our custom scenario.

The root cause: DAS preselection uses semantic similarity to match a user's query against scenario and capability descriptions. If the descriptions are too generic, they won't score high enough to beat the built-in capabilities like smalltalkCapability.

How to diagnose: Check the Joule debug logs and look for RAGE_SEARCHFILTER / RESULTS_FOUND. If only smalltalkCapability and px_user_feedback_capability appear in the results, the scenario isn't making it past preselection.

The fix: Enrich the descriptions with domain-specific keywords that users would naturally say:

# Too generic: loses to built-in capabilities
description: Answers questions about a document.

# Keyword-rich: wins preselection
description: >-
  Answers questions about project documents including requirements,
  architecture, data model, interfaces, personas, security,
  assumptions, and scope. Use when the user asks about project
  details, specifications, or design decisions.

Important: Update both the scenario YAML and capability.sapdas.yaml descriptions. Preselection checks both levels.

Issue 3: "Destination Not Found": The Cross-Subaccount Trap

W_API_REQUEST_EXECUTION errors with "destination not found."

The destination existed. We could see it in the BTP cockpit. But DAS couldn't find it.

The key insight: DAS runtime resolves destinations from its own subaccount, not from the application's subaccount. If the destination was only created in the app's subaccount, DAS will never see it.

The fix is twofold:

  1. Create a Destination Service instance (lite plan) in the Joule subaccount if one doesn't exist
  2. Create the destination in the Joule subaccount (or both subaccounts)

The destination configuration needs:

Property Value

NameThe destination name
TypeHTTP
URLThe backend's public URL
ProxyTypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2ClientCredentials
Client IDFrom XSUAA service key
Client SecretFrom XSUAA service key
Token Service URLThe subdomain's authentication token endpoint

Issue 4: The .internal Route Problem

Even with the destination correctly configured, we got 404 Not Found with x-cf-routererror: unknown_route.

The culprit: MTA deploys Cloud Foundry apps with .internal.cfapps. routes by default. These internal routes are not reachable across subaccounts. Since DAS runs in a different subaccount than the backend, it simply cannot reach an internal route.

Quick fix: map a public route:

cf map-route <app-name>-srv cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com \
  --hostname <app-hostname>

Verify it's reachable (a 401 means reachable but requires auth: that's correct):

curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" \
  "https://<hostname>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/<api-path>/$metadata"

Then update the destination URL to use the public route (without .internal).

Issue 5: Routes Disappearing After Redeploy

We fixed the route, everything worked, and then after the next cf deploy: Joule broke again with the same 404 unknown_route.

The reason: cf deploy resets routes to only those defined in mta.yaml. Our manually mapped public route was wiped out.

The permanent fix: add the public route to mta.yaml:

- name: <app-name>-srv
  type: nodejs
  path: gen/srv
  parameters:
    routes:
    - route: ${default-host}.internal.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
    - route: ${default-host}.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com   # add this

This ensures the public route survives every deployment.

Issue 6: Works Standalone, Fails in Work Zone

The final issue was the most confusing. Our capability worked perfectly in standalone Joule (/webclient/standalone/<bot-name>), but embedded Joule in SAP Build Work Zone couldn't find any of our scenarios.

The root cause: Work Zone uses the sap_digital_assistant assistant: a system-managed assistant that SAP products (Work Zone, SuccessFactors, etc.) share. Custom assistants created via joule deploy are completely separate.

Diagnosis: Run joule get sap_digital_assistant and check if the capability is listed. If it only shows smalltalkCapability and px_user_feedback_capability, the capability isn't registered.

The fix:

# Register the capability with the Work Zone assistant
joule update sap_digital_assistant --capability-file core/capability.sapdas.yaml

# Verify it's registered
joule get sap_digital_assistant

This was a critical "aha" moment: deploying to a custom assistant is great for testing, but for production use via Work Zone, explicit registration with sap_digital_assistant is required.

The Subaccount Mental Model

One of the biggest sources of confusion was understanding which component lives where. Here's the mapping that finally made everything click:

Component Subaccount Purpose

Backend Service (CAP app)Application subaccountBackend API + DB
Downstream ServicesApplication subaccountAI, processing, etc.
DAS / Joule RuntimeJoule subaccountCapability hosting
Build Work ZoneApplication subaccountUI / Launchpad
Backend DestinationBoth subaccountsDAS → backend connectivity
SWZ_JOULE DestinationApplication subaccountWork Zone → DAS connection

The key takeaway: destinations must exist where they're consumed, not where the target lives. DAS consumes the backend destination, so it must exist in the Joule subaccount.

Essential Commands Reference

For anyone working with DAS, these are the commands needed daily:

# Compile the capability (catches YAML errors early)
joule compile core

# Deploy to a standalone assistant for testing
joule deploy -c -n <bot-name>

# Register with Work Zone's assistant for production
joule update sap_digital_assistant --capability-file core/capability.sapdas.yaml

# List all assistants and inspect them
joule list
joule get sap_digital_assistant
joule get <bot-name>

# Launch standalone Joule for quick testing
joule launch <bot-name>

# Check and manage routes
cf app <app-name>-srv | grep routes
cf map-route <app-name>-srv cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com --hostname <hostname>

Lessons Learned

  • Compile early, compile often. DAS has strict but poorly documented validation rules. Don't wait until deployment to find out the descriptions are too long.
  • Think in subaccounts. Every cross-subaccount interaction: destinations, routes, assistant registration: is a potential failure point. Map out which components live where before starting.
  • Preselection is semantic, not exact. Scenario descriptions are effectively marketing copy for an AI classifier. They should be optimized like search keywords: domain-specific and descriptive.
  • Internal routes are invisible across subaccounts. Always add public routes to mta.yaml to survive redeployments.
  • Standalone ≠ Work Zone. Testing in standalone Joule doesn't validate the Work Zone integration. Always test in both contexts.
  • Debug logs are essential. The RAGE_SEARCHFILTER and RESULTS_FOUND entries reveal exactly what preselection is seeing.

Conclusion

Building a custom SAP Joule DAS integration involves more moving parts. The chain from user query to backend response crosses subaccount boundaries, networking layers, and semantic matching: each with its own failure modes.

The issues we encountered: compilation limits, preselection tuning, cross-subaccount destinations, internal routing, route persistence, and assistant registration, are not edge cases. They're the standard path anyone extending Joule will walk.

entry.title: New AI assistance for innovation adoption available now
entry.content:
SAP’s new AI assistance for innovation adoption represents a strategic evolution in customer service. This entitlement is the instrument which delivers the AI-driven quality and process guidance element within the Advanced and Max Success Plans of the SAP Services and Support Portfolio. 

It is designed to help organizations achieve operational excellence and innovation readiness across SAP’s integrated cloud solutions. Unlike traditional project-based or daily operational support, this assistance focuses on enabling continuous improvement through automated situation assessments and intelligent insights. 

 

  • Strategic AI-driven support: SAP’s AI assistance for innovation adoption is a strategic evolution in customer service, delivering AI-driven quality and process guidance as part of the Advanced and Max Success Plans within the SAP Services and Support portfolio.
  • Continuous improvement focus: Unlike traditional project-based or day-to-day operational support, this assistance enables ongoing optimization through automated situation assessments and intelligent, data-driven insights.
  • Operational excellence and innovation readiness: It is designed to help organizations achieve measurable improvements across SAP’s integrated cloud solutions, supporting both operational excellence and readiness for future innovation.
  • Three dimensions of customer value: Customers gain value through optimization (cost reduction and efficiency gains), improvement (upskilling teams with guided actions), and collaboration (shifting from passive consumption to active engagement).
  • Community-driven optimization model: The offering can be seen as an “optimization and improvement studio,” where participants actively influence shared expertise and continuously benefit from collective enhancements.

 

Scope
AI assistance for innovation adoption initially comprises a
library of six core capabilities that address critical areas of every
SAP solution:
• Data capacity excellence: Optimize data usage and storage.
• Evolution readiness: Prepare for future SAP innovations.
• Operational excellence of SAP BTP:
Improve platform operations.
• Enduring cybersecurity: Align security with business priorities.
• Extensibility intelligence: Reduce technical debt and
maintain a cleaner core.
• Adoption potential for RISE with SAP: Take full advantage of
all solution entitlements.

All capabilities are brought to life through engaging data
visualization, structured content, automated assessments, and
detailed actionable recommendations.

 

How it Works
The offering follows an Insight-to-Action-to-Outcome model:
• Insight: Automated data collection and identification of
relevant improvement opportunities.
• Action: Recommendations supported by structured follow-up
based on project and task templates.
• Outcome: Tangible improvements that compound over time
across solution areas.
You will find your cockpit to drive all outcomes in SAP Cloud ALM
for Service where various applications are seamlessly integrated
to enable this process. While customer consumption is
autonomous and mainly interactive, SAP’s data-driven processing is
fully automated, ensuring consistent quality, scalability, and
efficiency.
AI assistance for innovation adoption is offered as a content
subscription entitlement under the Advanced and Max Success
Plans. It requires a cloud application subscription and is technically
rendered for the integrated end-to-end solution.

 

Customer Impact
AI assistance for innovation adoption empowers you to achieve:
• Improved utilization of SAP features and functions.
• Operational cost reductions and efficiency gains.
• Enhanced self-sufficiency and transformation-readiness.
• Accelerated innovation adoption and security alignment.

 

Do you have questions or need more information?

Watch our introduction video
Check our Help Portal

Shape the offering’s future via SAP Continuous Influence,

be part of the SAP Customer Influence and Adoption program

Contact the AI assistance team via our shared mailbox

entry.title: Re: Issue with Migration Transfer Date and Fiscal Year Cl...
entry.content:
Good day @AlvaroFernandezMorales 

Thank you for your question.

What you described with error AC199, when fiscal year closing is checked against the latest legacy data transfer data in Asset Accounting is well documented in KBA 3730290, which describes the same symptom in the Make Company Code Settings-Asset Accounting Specific app.

Your current setup is last closed fiscal year = 2023 and your migration transfer date in the legacy data transfer segment = 31.12.2025.

Note that, for a transfer during the fiscal year, the transfer date must be in the fiscal year that directly follows the last closed fiscal year. The system derives the current fiscal year and the last closed fiscal year from the legacy data transfer date. Based on that, a transfer date of 31.12.2025 is not compatible with a system where 2023 is still the last closed fiscal year.

Below are references to help you make sense of it.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/1fd2035773b15632e10000000a441470.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/290e97c27ba3416cb7254cabe4d5dec4.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/cb476007e11c4221b22d87b80ed18c6d.html?locale=en-US

So, in principle, if 2023 is the last closed fiscal year, the valid migration setup must first be consistent with 2024 as the immediately following fiscal year.

Your understanding is correct about the legacy data transfer segments date and the system hindering your from moving backwards to an earlier transfer date. This is a limitation the Public Cloud documents in SAP Notes 2891851, 3408000 and 3724837, which includes the handling of completed and ongoing legacy transfer segments.

In addition, for your exact AC199 error, the issue lies in the coding of the fiscal year closing validation in the app. This is why the app can block you even though you are trying to move forward with the migration design. See KBA 3730290 for more details.

 

To resolve the issue, do not continue trying to close the fiscal year only in the Make Company Code Settings-Asset Accounting Specific App. Rather use the Schedule Asset Accounting Jobs app and run the job template as described in KBA 3730290. Same Notes also provides an alternative workaround.

A clean approach I would recommend for your test scenario with migration key date 31.12.2025 is as follows:

  1. Close Asset Accounting fiscal years in sequence until the system is consistent for the intended transfer design.
  2. Use Schedule Asset Accounting Jobs for the year end close instead of relying only on the company code settings app.
  3. Then make sure you recheck the legacy data transfer segment after you close.
  4. If you created 31.12.2025 segment prematurely and your system design is no longer consistent, then review whether you need to:
    1. Keep it and continue the required year closures in sequence, or
    2. Reset incorrect test data in a nonproduction tenant and create the migration setup correctly. See KBA 3728565 for more details.

See the following references for more insights.

3224015 - Error AC199 during AJAB - SAP for Me

3318060 - Central KBA Legacy Data Transfer in Asset Accounting S/4HANA / How-to scenarios, standard behavior and guides. - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP/CCM - CCS USAGE = 5 Settlement condition with acc...
entry.content:
If you want RES1 to drive settlement value, it cannot be flagged as “Accruals” in V/06. Instead, you should separate the roles:

  • Keep RES1 purely as accrual condition (with accruals flag).
  • Use a dedicated rebate condition (e.g., RES2) without accruals flag for settlement net value.
  • Map RES1 → RED2 for accrual postings, and RES2 → settlement for payout calculation.

This way, you maintain clean separation and accrual postings flow via RES1/RED2, while settlement amounts flow via RES2. Trying to use one condition type for both accruals and settlement net value will always lead to the “zero net value” issue what you are seeing.

entry.title: Re: Billing Plan Restrictions in Quantity Contracts
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, rental/periodic billing contracts are best represented either through Quantity Contracts with item-level billing plans or, more flexibly, via SAP Subscription Billing integrated with S/4HANA Cloud. Header-level billing plans and custom billing plan types are not available in Public Cloud; item-level billing plans with fixed type S5 are the standard.

Having said that, the recommended approach is

  • For basic rental contracts → Use Quantity Contracts with item-level billing plans (S5).
  • For advanced subscription/rental models → Implement SAP Subscription Billing integrated with S/4HANA Cloud.
  • Align item categories with billing plan requirements to avoid manual adjustments.

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Apps Deprecation and The Path Forward
entry.content:
@patrick_schad I would like to reiterate the question from @capabilityworks :

"Is this basically the removal of SAP Build Apps?  SAP Build has very basic form based UI capabilities and process, workflows and RPA automation capabilities. SAP Business Application Studio/VSCode are needed for SAPUI5, the Flexible Programming  Model, Fiori Elements and CAP and ADT Eclipse for RAP ABAP based logic, services and CDS development. Key to understanding a unified development approach is clarity of the role of SAP Build alongside SAP BAS and ADT and how SAP Build Code and Joule fit. Is there a view of how these multiple tools will work together."

I would like to express my dissatisfaction with the way SAP handles its technologies. Unfortunately, this is not the first time that solutions presented as “future-proof” have later been discontinued. Customers invest significant time and money into building applications based on these technologies, trusting in their long-term viability.

However, when such technologies are suddenly deprecated, customers are forced to invest additional resources to adapt or rebuild their solutions—despite not being responsible for the situation. This creates uncertainty and makes it difficult to rely on SAP’s long-term technology strategy.

entry.title: Re: Can the Cost Month end process automated starting fro...
entry.content:
Yes, it is possible to automate the sequence of cost month‑end transactions you listed in ECC (F.13, KSU5, KSS2, KSII, CON2, S_ALR_87013611, KKA0/KKAO, KKS5, CO88, CKMLCP, CKM3N). The key is to orchestrate them through background jobs or process chains rather than executing each transaction manually.

Use the Closing Cockpit to define your month‑end process flow. Assign each transaction as a task with variants, link them in the required sequence, and schedule them. This gives you automation, monitoring, and auditability in one place.

entry.title: Re: Deactivation of Company Code
entry.content:
In SAP ECC and S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, deactivating a company code does not delete its data. Historical data remains in the system for as long as retention rules require, and it stays accessible to authorized users for audit and compliance purposes. In Public Cloud, archiving is supported but happens within the same system, using SAP’s standard data archiving framework.

So what you can explore is archiving is supported in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition via the Data Archiving Framework. Archived data is stored within the same system and it is not moved to an external system by default. The process is managed through the Manage Archiving Variants app, where you define retention periods, blocking rules, and destruction rules. Archived data remains accessible (read-only) until the retention period expires, ensuring compliance with audit requirements.

entry.title: Re: VAT ID x country mismatch: impact on tax determinatio...
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, a VAT ID is only treated as valid if its country prefix matches the business partner’s country. If there’s a mismatch, the system excludes it from tax determination and reporting (e.g., EC Sales List), which is by design to ensure compliance with EU VAT rules.

Having said that, the recommendation is stick with SAP’s standard validation to remain compliant. If customers have multiple VAT registrations, model them as separate business partners or tax numbers. For document output, you can customize forms to display the VAT ID, but reporting will still follow compliance rules.

Coming to impact on VAT reporting is EC Sales List requires the VAT ID of the partner to match their country. If mismatched, the transaction is reported as domestic (with VAT) rather than intra‑EU supply (zero VAT).

entry.title: Useful steps for admins when migrating from SuccessFactors Legacy People Profile to Full Profile
entry.content:
With Full Profile of the New People Profile on the horizon and being a mandatory change for 1H 2026, here are some practical/admin tips for your transition from Legacy People Profile (LPP) to Full Profile (FP)

1. Clean-up your LPP before running the FP migration job.

Remove any unnecessary, empty, or hidden sections, sub-sections, and blocks before starting the migration. This ensures that the migration job includes only the required portlets, resulting in a cleaner FP environment and eliminating the need for post-migration cleanup.

There are chances that admins may have created these unwanted sections for some testing purposes in the past but never permissioned. So, do a check before initiating the migration job.

2. Understand what is supported and not supported in the FP and how do you identify what migrated across to the FP and what left behind?

Some portlets of LPP such as mini-org chart, performance-potential matrix, functional experience etc. are not supported in FP. So, even when you run the migration job, they are not migrated over to the FP. Therefore, it is important to know what got migrated and not when you ran the job.

How do identify what migrated across to the FP in your system?

When you run the FP migration job from Company Systems & Logo settings page, extract the Download Status file from the Scheduled Job Manager. This gives a comprehensive list of what got migrated and what was left out. It helps you understand old blocks vs new cards in your system. Some screenshots below:

Scheduled Job Manager

Sample of log file

Note: If you have already converted to FP and have done some work on FP, re-running the Migration job would wipe off your FP work and make it a clean slate. So, do it with due diligence.

If you want to switch back to old profile for any testing, you can switch back by unticking the ‘Enable Full Profile for the Latest People Profile’ and revert to FP again by ticking it. This way, your FP work won’t erase.

 

3. Work on the FP Layout:

Once you get a clean transition, and only with wanted categories & cards, now, it is up to the customers to work on the layout. Obviously, you will have an option to rearrange categories and some cards across & within categories. Work on this in consultation with your business and make it suitable as per your business requirements.

Depending on your requirements, you may have to work on RBPs’ here and what you want to see on the cards. You may have to do some RBP tweaks and ordering of the fields so that the most useful information remains on top of the card without needing to dig into the card. (wear a confidentiality lens when doing this)

4. Understand the two-layer permissions:

While your original RBP drives the access to the fields, your category level permissions drives visibility to the categories. Using a RBP Permissions vs Roles Table Report, you can identify which roles has access to the sections/employee views in the LPP and mimic the permissions for Categories in the FP.

For example, from the above-mentioned table report, you identified that the Compensation section (Employee View) in the LPP is permissioned for X, Y, Z roles. Now, in the FP, you can provide the same roles (X, Y, Z) access to the Compensation category and this way you are ensuring that the relevant roles have access to the categories in the FP. (Once you start working on this, you will get a hang on this. If you need any help on the report, feel free to contact me and happy to help).

5. Use Configuration Transport Centre (CTC) to migrate your changes across SF instances. (This is truly a life saver)

Believe me, it is indeed a life saver!

Assuming you have started & developed all your work in a test instance before moving to production, using a CTC will save your time, effort and an efficient way to copy your changes across the instances.

Through CTC, your entire FP config will copy across the instances. The ordering of the categories, cards, category permissions, any text changes config will all copy across.

In my experience, the config in the General settings i.e., Header fields, enable latest Editing & History UI etc. of the FP did not copy across, but these are just couple of ticks, so that worries least for any admins/customers. You can manually tick them once your category level config is copied over.

6. Finally, some quick unit testing to make sure everything is done properly!

entry.title: Re: Removal of legacy sap.ui.webc.* Web Component UI libraries from SAPUI5/OpenUI5 1.147.0 onwards
entry.content:
Hi Community,

"

If you used sap.ui.webc.* in a prototype or experiment: Since these libraries were always experimental, now is the time to move to the new approach. The controls you were using (like sap.ui.webc.main.Button or sap.ui.webc.fiori.ShellBar) have no 1:1 migration path because the consumption model changed fundamentally. However, the underlying Web Components are the same — and better, since you now get v2.x instead of v1.18.

If you used sap.ui.webc.* in a productive application despite the experimental status: The libraries will be removed in version 1.147.0. We recommend starting the migration now. The Using Web Components guide is your starting point.

If you're on the 1.120 LTS and planning to upgrade to the next LTS: The previous LTS (1.120) still ships the deprecated sap.ui.webc.* libraries. The next LTS (1.148) will not — they are removed one version earlier in 1.147. If your application depends on these libraries, you must migrate before upgrading to 1.148 LTS.

"

 

Thanks for your selfless sharing again, it really help us a lot, right now.

🙂

 

Regards,

ZFiori.

entry.title: Re: Your 2026 Roadmap to Getting Started with ABAP AI and ABAP-1
entry.content:
Excellent roadmap post  the framing of three distinct paths (Daily Developer, Innovation, and Modernization) is exactly the clarity the ABAP community needs right now because the noise around ABAP-1 has created a lot of confusion about where to actually start.

The key takeaway is spot on: don't get bogged down trying to prompt engineer experimental models like ABAP-1 for production code generation  leverage Joule for Developers' mature Explain and Generate skills to maintain a Clean Core and accelerate migration to SAP S/4HANA. That's the practical advice most developers need to hear.

The VS Code with ABAP Cloud path planned for Q2 2026 with built-in Agentic AI assistance powered by an ABAP MCP server is the one I'm watching most closely — supporting a broader range of SAP S/4HANA Private Editions with faster update cycles addresses a real gap for customers who aren't on Public Cloud.

One adjacent point worth raising as teams move down the Modernization path: before migrating to ABAP Cloud or S/4HANA, organizations need a clear handle on what SAP data they're carrying forward and what should be archived or retired. Years of accumulated SAP data  financial documents, sales orders, HR records  need to be managed as part of the migration strategy, not after it. Solutions like Solix ILM for SAP help enterprises archive, retain, and govern that data properly so what lands in the clean core system is lean, compliant, and AI-ready from day one.

One question worth raising: for organizations with large volumes of classic ABAP custom code, is there a recommended pattern for prioritizing which custom code objects to migrate first  beyond the standard ATC checks  particularly when business logic is deeply intertwined with classic enhancement frameworks like user exits and BADIs that have no direct ABAP Cloud equivalent?

entry.title: The initial replication for demands in Professional Services Projects
entry.content:
We recently met several askings for what would be initial replicated from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition-S4HC to SAP Project to Resource Management - Resource Management(SPRM-RM) system? How to associate the created resource requests in RM with demands under a Professional Services project in S4HC?

I have tested in my internal system and documented it here for your reference.

In S4HC system, I create one customer project and create demands for roles T001, T002, T003, T004:

After 15 minutes' sync interval, these demands are replicated to RM system and create resource requests.
You can check in "Manage Resource Requests" app, and add 'Project' 'Work Package' within the Adapt Filters will help you quickly capture the demands you created in S4HC.
On the top right corner of the result lists, there is a Setting button.
You can add columns such as 'Demand', 'Work Item', 'Requested Resource Organization', 'Required Effort' to help you associate the resource request in RM to the demands in S4HC.

Then check the column 'Publishing Status', if it's Not Published, the resource manager can update the resource org or any other fields here directly by Edit this resource request:

However, if you set Auto Publish in the replication job, the resource request has already Published, you can still go to "Staff Resource Requests" app to forward the resource org:

After Forward, the 'Requested Resource Organization' is keeping to the original one, the 'Processing Resource Organization' will be the new one, 'Matching Resources' will by default based on the processing resource org.

entry.title: Re: Rethink ALM: Moving to SAP Cloud ALM as a Clean Core Strategy for Application Lifecycle Manageme
entry.content:
Really well-framed post  the distinction between migrating a tool versus redesigning your ALM function is exactly the mindset shift that most teams miss. SAP Cloud ALM isn't meant to do everything SAP Solution Manager did  and that's actually the point. Customers moving to SAP Cloud ALM must step back and evaluate which ALM processes are truly needed and which are not. That's uncomfortable for teams who've spent years building Solution Manager configurations, but it's the right conversation to have.

The Joule integration angle makes this even more compelling  Joule assists in automatically generating project requirements from Fit-to-Standard workshop transcripts, and Joule Agents now automate routine clean core tasks such as managing quality gates and performing completeness checks for governance questionnaires. That changes the ROI calculation significantly for teams that are still manually managing these processes in Solution Manager.

One point worth emphasizing from the field: clean core is not a one-time configuration it requires ongoing coordination, process discipline, and the right tools at the right time. SAP Cloud ALM as the "connective tissue" between business requirements and technical implementations is the right framing, but it only holds if teams resist the temptation to recreate legacy Solution Manager complexity inside the new tool.

One adjacent consideration that often gets missed in ALM discussions: before Cloud ALM governance can work effectively, the underlying data landscape needs to be in order. ECC environments frequently carry decades of accumulated data — financial records, procurement history, HR documents  that hasn't been classified, archived, or rationalized. Migrating that data volume into S/4HANA without a structured archiving strategy inflates infrastructure costs, extends testing cycles, and increases reconciliation risk. Solix has published a detailed architectural decision framework specifically around this data lifecycle challenge for large-scale SAP migrations, which is worth reading alongside any Cloud ALM planning exercise.

One practical question: for organizations that have heavily customized Solution Manager's ChaRM process for change management  with custom workflows, approval steps, and integrations  what's the recommended approach to rationalizing those before migrating to Cloud ALM's change and deployment capabilities? Is the expectation a clean-slate redesign, or is there a migration accelerator that preserves meaningful governance logic without carrying over the complexity?

entry.title: How Agentic AI Slashes Parallel Payroll Reconciliation from Weeks to Days
entry.content:
The Last Mile That Matters Most: Parallel Payroll Reconciliation

Every SAP SuccessFactors ECP implementation builds toward one critical milestone: the parallel payroll phase. It's often one of the last major phases of the project, and a crucial one. Teams frequently spend extended hours, sometimes working round the clock, to analyze comparison reports and identify the root causes of discrepancies. These can range from data issues and configuration gaps to data transformation problems such as mapping errors between Legacy and SAP.

Here's how it works: the implementation team takes a snapshot of several Legacy pay periods, loads the same employee data and configuration into SAP ECP, runs the payroll in ECP, and then compares the results. The question is simple: Does SAP calculate the same result as Legacy?

When it doesn't, and it often doesn't, the real pain begins. A single discrepancy at the Taxable Gross level (/3xx) cascades through the entire tax chain, creating a waterfall of mismatches that obscures the actual root cause.

What AI Does Differently: Reasoning, Not Just Flagging

Traditional reconciliation tools give you a spreadsheet of red and green cells. The Parallel Payroll Agent goes far beyond detection , it reasons about every discrepancy.

What We Built : The AI Understands the Tax Chain

We built an AI-powered Parallel Payroll Reconciliation Agent that validates across all payroll components earnings (regular pay, overtime, bonuses etc ), deductions (401k, FSA, HSA, dental, vision, medical etc ), imputed income (GTL, personal use of company car etc ), and employer-paid benefits , each traced through the full cascading tax chain per tax authority.

Consider Employee 0001 with the following payroll breakdown:

With this breakdown, the /301 VA Taxable Gross should be $4,250.00 (Earnings $4,150 + Imputed Income $100). The /601 VA Taxable Base should be $3,840.00 (Taxable Gross $4,250 minus pre-tax deductions $410). Now let's see what the agent finds when comparing Legacy vs SAP:

The agent identifies that the $70 difference at /601 VA (Taxable Base) is actually caused by two completely different issues: $100 cascading from the /301 VA GTL Imputed Income problem, offset by $30 from an independent FSA configuration error. It labels this as "Mixed (Cascade + Root)" and explains both components.

Before vs. After: The Transformation

How It Works: Smart Architecture, Minimal Token Usage

We designed the agent in a way that the vast majority of discrepancies are resolved by domain rules using the fewest tokens possible to deliver the best output. AI is invoked for the long tail of genuinely unexplained edge cases.This keeps costs low while delivering expert-level analysis.

Solving Multi-Combination Puzzles

One of the hardest challenges in payroll reconciliation is when a single dollar gap can be explained by multiple deduction combinations. For example:

 

The agent tests every single deduction, every pair, and every triple against the employee's gap for the pattern, and it is confirmed: FSA is the root cause.

Real Results: A Live Validation Run

Here's what the agent produced for a real parallel payroll dataset with 2 employees and 102 wage type records across Federal and Virginia tax authorities:

Notice how the AI distinguishes between root causes (fix these) and cascade effects (these resolve automatically once root causes are fixed). This alone saves consultants from chasing phantom issues.

Time and Cost Savings

SAP ECP implementations are complex. Parallel payroll reconciliation is often the time-consuming and labor-intensive phase of the project, not because the work is intellectually difficult, but because the volume is overwhelming. AI doesn't replace the payroll consultant's, operations, or tax team expertise. It amplifies it. The team still makes the final call, but now they're reviewing AI-generated explanations instead of building them from scratch.

This is what Agentic AI looks like in enterprise payroll: not a chatbot, not a dashboard, a domain-specific reasoning engine that understands tax chain dependencies, deduction decomposition, and cross-employee patterns.

If you're in the SAP SuccessFactors ECP space and want to hear how this works, I'd love to connect.

*Claude Parallel Payroll Agent plugin, submitted and pending approval from Anthropic.

entry.title: Re: ABAP test cockpit (ATC) recommendations for governance of clean core ABAP development
entry.content:
Excellent and detailed post  the shift from the 3-tier extensibility model to the Clean Core Level Concept is exactly the kind of practical reframing that teams needed. The A–D level framework gives teams a structured classification for every extension and a consistent way to manage the associated upgrade risk  Level A for fully upgrade-safe ABAP Cloud extensions, Level B for classic but documented APIs with governance sign-off, Level C for internal objects on a documented remediation roadmap, and Level D for modifications that should carry a retirement timeline. That clarity was missing from the binary clean/not-clean model.
The point about ATC exemption logs is particularly important in practice  an exemption log that grows without regular review is one of the clearest early indicators of governance drift, and tracking exemption volume and approval rationale as a Clean Core KPI catches that trend before it becomes a landscape-level problem. Most teams treat exemptions as a one-way door and never revisit them.
The recommendation to use ATC on SAP BTP as the central check system connected across all development-related systems  with blocking mode active for Priority 1 and 2 findings on transport tasks is the right architecture for any team serious about governance at scale. The inclusion of SAP CVA security checks without separate licensing is also a meaningful practical benefit that often gets overlooked in the conversation.
One question worth raising: for organizations with large volumes of Level C and D code identified after the initial ATC readiness run, what's the recommended approach to sequencing the remediation roadmap  is the guidance to tackle the highest-frequency objects first, the highest-risk upgrade objects first, or to align remediation with the business process migration sequence in the S/4HANA project plan?

entry.title: Re: CLM - Contract and Lease Management Migration in Publ...
entry.content:
Good day @SonyaM 

Thank you for your question.

Based on the details you shared, the issue is not the migration cockpit itself. What you are experiencing is caused by the valuation input data of your specific contract. In your case, the condition, the condition with valuation property Present Value Specified is higher than the contract value, and this leads exactly to the negative interest calculation errors you listed, including RECEPR151 and the related valuation inconsistencies.

What is important in the case you described is, when you migrate a lease contract with one-time statistical condition NPV Default using Present Value Specified, the system does not use the interest rate your manually entered from the valuation parameters as the effective driver for the valuation. See KBA 3041660 for more details.

So, if the contract you migrated has Present Value Specified = 96,898.99 and Correct Value = 76,762.25, then the present value is already greater than the value of the future lease payments. See Notes 3531206  for more insights on that exact setup producing a negative calculated interest rate from inconsistent valuation input.

So, yes, the behaviour you experience is expected when Present Value Specified exceeds Contract Value. The system is not ignoring a valid setup. It is rejecting an inconsistent one.

You must correct the Present Value Specified so that it is equal to or lower than the contract value for the lease payments considered in valuation. In your example, 96,898.99 cannot be used together with a contract value of 76,762.25. I suggest you fix this first.

For your type of migrated contract, you should make the correction in the migrated valuation input itself, meaning in the statistical migration condition that carries Presen Value Specified, not try to force the valuation through the interest rate field. See KBA 3041660 for more details.

For this contract a practical migration approach would be for you to:

  1. Review the original source migration data for the Asset Default Value and Net Present Value Default conditions.
  2. Verify whether the NPV amount was loaded incorrectly, or whether the contract value basis is incomplete, for example missing or reduced lease cash flows.
  3. Correct the contract so that the Present Value Specified condition is no greater than the contract value.
  4. Re run valuation simulation only after that correction.

If your contract was already migrated incorrectly, then I would recommend the following references:

3409502 - Process to perform early termination on contracts which have inconsistent data due to incorrect initial migration - SAP for Me

2544232 - Leasing: Handling transition scenarios - SAP for Me

 

To conclude, my recommendation/the decision to consider is:

  • Either you adjust the Present Value Specified downward, or
  • You correct the underlying lease cash flows upward if the contract value is currently understated.      

What I would not advice is to rely on the manually entered interest rate to compensate for that mismatch, because that is not how the valuation logic works when present value is specified.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: How to Avoid the Most Common Account Assignment Object issues in SAP Asset Accounting (FI-AA)
entry.content:
Working in SAP Asset Accounting (FIAA) often means dealing with recurring and sometimes puzzling error messages such as:

  • GLT2201
  • AAPO189 / AAPO190
  • FAA_POST042

Although these messages may appear unrelated, they all share a common root cause:

👉issues in the configuration or derivation of Additional Account Assignment Objects during FI-AA postings.

In this blog, you’ll learn why these errors occur, how to analyse them, and most importantly how to prevent them through proper configuration.

 

🧩 1. What Are Account Assignment Objects in Asset Accounting?

In SAP FI-AA, each asset can carry specific account assignment information used during postings. These objects fall into two main categories:

1.1 Time-Dependent Account Assignment Objects 

(stored in table ANLZ)

These include:

    • Internal order (CAUFN)

    • WBS element (Costs) (PS_PSP_PNR2)
    • Cost center (KOSTL)
    • Profit Center (PRCTR)
    • …

They are used exclusively during:

    • Depreciation postings

    • Gain/loss postings
    • Interest postings

📌 Important:  They are not used for APC (Acquisition and Production Cost) postings. Therefore, these objects must point to real cost elements, not statistical ones.

1.2 Account Assignment Objects for APC Postings

APC postings require statistical objects, such as:

    • Investment Order (ANLA-EAUFN)

    • WBS element (ANLA-PS_PSP_PNR)
    • Cost Center (ANLZ-KOSTL)
    • …

These objects depend on statistical cost elements (category 90).

📌 Important:   To allow this, you must ensure system message KI098 is set to off or warning in transaction OBA5.

 

⚙️2. Key Configuration Steps in Asset Accounting

2.1 Activate Account Assignment Objects

SPRO path: 

Financial Accounting → Asset Accounting → Integration with General Ledger → Additional Account Assignment Objects → Activate Account Assignment Objects

Note: If you activate the Identical flag, SAP enforces that the account assignment object posted in the G/L document must match the one existing in the asset master.

2.2 Specify Account Assignment Types

SPRO path:

Financial Accounting → Asset Accounting → Integration with General Ledger → Additional Account Assignment Objects → Activate Account Assignment Objects → Specify Account Assignment Type for Account Assignment Objects

Here you define which objects are active for:

  • APC values posting
  • Depreciation run
  • Relevant transaction types

📌 Best practice: Enable both APC and Depreciation settings for all transaction types.

2.3 Check if the validity of the Account Assignment Object

Ex: Cost Center (Transaction KS03): 

2.4 Account Assignment Object fields active in the Asset Master Data, to be derived.

Always ensure that the Account Assignment Object is set as Optional in the Asset Master Screen Layout

In case of PROFIT CENTER:

  • If Segment Reporting is active, you have Profit Center field in the Asset Master Data, Time-Dependent Tab.
    Profit Center must be set as Optional in the Asset Master Screen Layout
  • If Segment Reporting is not active, Profit Center can be derived from:
    •  Cost Center (ANLZ-KOSTL).
    •  WBS (ANLA-PS_PSP_PNR) or ORDER (ANLA-EAUFN)
    • Substitution
    • Default Account Assignment OKB9

       In these cases, points 2.1, 2.2 and 2.3 must be fulfilled and maintained in the Asset Master Data.

2.5 G/L Account configuration:

The correct functioning of account assignment derivation depends heavily on how G/L accounts and field controls are configured.

  1. Check whether the Asset Reconciliation accounts (B/S account) manage CO account assignments statistically:
    --> In S/4HANA system: Record Account Assignment flag in FS00 transaction (Control Data tab)


--> In ECC system: Account should have a Cost Element with Statistical Cost Element Category (90)

 

Note that for accounts of cost element category 11 "Revenues" or 12 "Sales Deductions", the objects from the asset master record are only assigned if revenue  posting is allowed in the corresponding CO object.

For instance, for the cost center to be derived it must not be blocked for revenues in transaction KS03:

 

2. Make sure that the account assignment objects are ready for input in the field status of the G/L account and the field status of the posting keys. Hidden objects are not copied to the line items. (Example: If you grey out the Cost Center in the field status, a Profit Center derivation cannot be performed for this line item).

 

🚨Understanding the Most Common FI-AA Errors

Here’s a clear explanation of why each error happens.

🔸GLT2201 — “Balancing field & in line item & not filled”

Cross-application error that is due to a wrong derivation of the Account Assignment objects that intervene in the posting of a FI document.

Occurs because in the "Document splitting" customizing, you have indicated the Profit Center as "Zero balance" and "Mandatory". However, you are still not deriving a Profit Center from the asset master in relation to asset line items posted in the FI-GL document.

SPRO: Financial Accounting--> General Ledger Accounting --> Document Splitting--> Define Document Splitting Characteristics for General Ledger Accounting occurs when:

 

🔸AAPO189 / AAPO190 — “Account object &1 differs from that in asset master record"

Occurs because an account assignment object that differs from the asset master record was entered in the document, because:

  • It is not derived from the Asset Master record
  • There is a substitution, exits or BTE that may impact the account assignment derivation
  • Different Account Assignment Object from an investment measure (WBS, order)

This check is done when the “identical” indicator is set as explained in point 2.1

🔸FAA_POST042 — “Balance in techn. clearing account: ledger &1, currency &2, &3, &4"

Occurs when posting an integrated business transaction (for instance, an integrated asset acquisition). The system splits the business transaction into an operational and a valuating document. Both documents are posted to account Technical Clearing Account for Integrated Asset Acquisition, which has to display a balance of zero at the account assignment objects level, otherwise transaction cannot be posted. 

  • Ensure that the Technical Clearing Account for Integrated Asset Acquisition (SPRO: Financial Accounting --> Asset Accounting--> Integration with General Ledger Accounting --> Technical Clearing Account for Integrated Asset Acquisition) and the fixed asset balance sheet account have the same field control.  
  • The Technical Clearing Account for Integrated Asset Acquisition must be able to manage CO account assignments statistically.
  • Ensure that no substitution, exits or BTE exist, that may impact the account assignment derivation.

 

🧠 5. Summary & Best Practices

To avoid these account assignment object errors:

✔ Activate relevant assignment objects in customizing

  • SPRO path: Financial Accounting → Asset Accounting → Integration with General Ledger → Additional Account Assignment Objects → Activate Account Assignment Objects
  • Transaction: ACSET

✔ Set the Account Assignment Objects as Optional in the different Layouts:

  • Asset Master Data Layout: SPRO path: Financial Accounting → Asset Accounting → Master Data →Screen Layout →Define Screen Layout for Asset Master Data
  • G/L Account Field Status Group: SPRO path → Financial Accounting → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Ledgers → Fields → Define Field Status Variant (OBC4) 
  • Posting Key Field Status: SPRO path→ Financial Accounting → General Ledger Accounting → Document Entry → Define Posting Keys (OB41)

 ✔ Maintain correct Account Assignment Objects in the Asset Master Data or alternative Object to be derived from.

✔ Check G/L accounts

  • Reconciliation accounts must allow statistical assignments
  • Ensure system message KI098 is set to off or warning in transaction OBA5

✔ Validate Account Assignment Objects

  • Cost Centers, Orders, WBS elements, etc, must be valid and active.

By ensuring these configuration elements are aligned across FI, CO, and AA, you can prevent the majority of FI-AA posting issues and maintain smooth asset-related financial processes.

entry.title: Centralized Error Handling in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP Cloud Integration, runtime errors can occur due to various reasons such as script failures, adapter issues, or unexpected payloads. Handling such errors individually in every integration flow often leads to duplicated logic and increased maintenance effort.

This blog demonstrates a centralized error handling mechanism in SAP Cloud Integration that:

  • Captures errors using an Exception Subprocess
  • Forwards error data using the ProcessDirect adapter
  • Sends HTML-formatted email notifications
  • Is reusable across multiple business integration flows

Solution

The solution consists of two types of integration flows:

1. Business Integration Flows

  • Executes business logic
  • Uses an Exception Subprocess to capture errors
  • Sends error details to a centralized logger

2. Central Error Logger Integration Flows

  • Receives error messages from multiple iFlows
  • Normalizes error data
  • Sends styled HTML email notifications to the support team

Step 1: Create the Business Integration Flow

1. Navigate to the SAP Integration Suite

2. Go to Design → Integrations and APIs

3. Create a new package (example: PCK_CentralErrorLogging)

4. Create a new Integration Flow named Business_Test_Iflow

This iFlow represents a sample business process where a runtime error occurs.

 

Step 2: Configure the Sender and Main Process

1. Add an HTTPS Sender Adapter

  • Configure endpoint address (example: /testError)
  • Configure authentication as required

2. Connect the adapter to the Start Event

3. Add a Groovy Script named Script_Groovy_Generate_Error

  • This script deliberately throws an exception for testing purposes

4. Connect the script to the End Event

 

Step 3: Add Exception Subprocess for Error Capture

1. Add an Exception Subprocess to the Business iFlow

2. Add a Groovy Script inside the subprocess named Script_Groovy_CaptureError

The Groovy script performs the following responsibilities:

  • Capture integration flow name
  • Capture message ID
  • Generate correlation ID
  • Capture error message
  • Generate timestamps (IST and UTC)

All values are stored as message properties. Using an Exception Subprocess enables centralized error capture without modifying individual processing steps.

 

Step 4: Send Error Details via ProcessDirect Adapter

1. After the Groovy script, add a ProcessDirect Receiver Adapter

2. Configure the address as /centralErrorLogger

This enables reusability across multiple business iFlows.

Step 5: Create the Central Error Logger Integration Flow

1. Create an integration flow named Central_Error_Logger_Iflow

2. Configure the Sender Adapter as ProcessDirect

3. Use the same address /centralErrorLogger

Step 6: Normalize Incoming Error Data

1. Add a Groovy Script as the first step

2. The script reads error details from properties, headers, or payload and applies fallback values if data is missing, preparing a consistent structure for email formatting.

This makes the central logger reusable.

Step 7: Format Error Details into HTML

1. Add a Content Modifier

2. Create an HTML table displaying:

  • Integration Flow Name
  • Message ID
  • Adapter Type
  • Error Message
  • Timestamp (IST and UTC)
  • Correlation ID

 

Step 8: Configure Email Notification

1. Add a Mail Receiver Adapter

2. Configure mail server, sender and receiver email IDs, subject, and set MIME type to text/html

Step 9: End-to-End Testing

1. Deploy both integration flows

2. Trigger the business iFlow using the HTTP endpoint

3. Verify the following:

  • Business iFlow succeeds in Message Monitor
  • Central Error Logger receives the error
  • HTML email is delivered with error details

 

Validation Using Another Business Integration Flow

The same centralized error handling was validated with another real-world integration flow named Invoice_Batch_Email_Processor_With_ProcessDirect.

  • Multiple processing steps
  • Routers based on message severity
  • Multiple email notifications
  • Groovy scripts and content modifiers

 

Key Observations

  • Exception Subprocess captured runtime errors successfully
  • The same central error logger iFlow was reused
  • No changes were required in the logger
  • HTML email alerts were generated correctly

 

This confirms the solution works for both simple and complex integration flows.

Benefits of This Approach

  • Centralized error handling
  • Reusable across multiple iFlows
  • Clean separation of business logic and error handling
  • Faster troubleshooting using Message ID and Correlation ID
  • Professional HTML email notifications
  • Proven in real-world scenarios

Conclusion

By implementing centralized error handling using Exception Subprocess and ProcessDirect adapters, SAP Cloud Integration landscapes become easier to maintain, monitor, and support. This approach reduces duplication, improves observability, and scales efficiently across integration designs.

This solution was implemented and tested in SAP Integration Suite, and all screenshots are from real execution to ensure accuracy and practical relevance.

entry.title: Re: Cross-Plant PPDS Planning: Special Procurement Not Tr...
entry.content:
To achieve your scenario, maintain a source list entry in Plant A pointing to Plant B, ensure transportation lane exists, and confirm PDS is active in Plant B. This is the standard fit‑to‑standard approach for cross‑plant PP/DS planning in S/4HANA.

Would you like me to outline a step‑by‑step configuration checklist (special procurement, source list, lane, PDS) so you can validate each piece systematically? That usually helps pinpoint why MRP Live skipped the STR creation.

entry.title: Re: Error When Confirming Transfer Order – “Enter a Stora...
entry.content: That error message “Enter a storage unit” usually points to the fact that the warehouse you are working in is storage unit–managed (SU‑managed). In SU‑managed warehouses, every transfer order confirmation requires you to specify the storage unit number, because the system tracks stock at the storage unit level rather than just at bin level. Start by checking whether your warehouse is SU‑managed. If yes, you’ll need to enter or generate storage unit numbers consistently during TO creation and confirmation. If not, review the movement type and warehouse settings to remove the SU requirement.

entry.title: Re: How Contractual delivery date for ADHOC line gets determined in sales order in SAP
entry.content: Instead of using Subject Text for your requirement, use the box where you have to explain clearly your requirement with related screen shots. Make use of the Subject title effectively and avoid lengthy text

entry.title: Re: Tabela de vínculo de número de série em esboço (Draft...
entry.content: As far as I know, and based on the SAP Business One database structure, the serial number assignment in draft documents is handled via table ODSR. It links the draft document (DocEntry) and its item lines (BaseLinNum) with the corresponding serial numbers (SysSerial).

entry.title: Re: Partial payments/invoice for 1 purchased pc
entry.content: For negotiated milestone payments (e.g., 30% on PO issue, 30% on engineering approval, 40% on receipt), SAP supports this via down payment requests or milestone billing plans on the PO. These payments are not tied to GR. Instead, they are controlled by the payment terms and billing plan defined in the PO. Finance can process these payments against the PO using the agreed billing plan, with clear audit trail that it is a contractual milestone, not a goods receipt.

entry.title: Re: PPDS Process Order Not Visible in Fiori Confirmation...
entry.content: This is expected behavior, as Fiori confirmation apps use operation/phase scheduled dates rather than header dates. The scheduling result can be analyzed in /SAPAPO/RRP3; possible influencing factors include capacity constraints, resource sequencing/setup, or material availability, depending on the PP/DS planning situation. After confirmation, detailed scheduling logs are generally not retained, so identifying the exact reason typically requires reproducing the scenario with scheduling trace/log enabled.

entry.title: Re: Check on partner roles in PO
entry.content: In standard SAP, there is not a direct configuration switch that enforces “only allow invoicing parties from BP master data” when you change the PI partner in a PO. The system will let you assign any BP with the PI role, unless you add a control layer. Having said that, you cannot achieve this purely with customizing. The fit‑to‑standard approach is to use partner determination for proposal plus a validation enhancement (BAdI/custom logic) to enforce the restriction.
